Things To Do

The whole North Fork,
from our front door.

South Jamesport sits at the heart of Long Island's wine and farm country — close enough to walk to dinner, central enough to reach every winery, beach, and village along the peninsula.

5min To Town
Steps Away

The village, just outside.

Take a stroll — most of South Jamesport is just a few minutes from your door.

Local restaurants, a friendly tavern, antique shops, the village green, and the public beach are all an easy stroll away. No car needed for an entire weekend if you don't want one.

Local Restaurants Boutique Shops Public Beach Village Green Marina
A Short Ride Away

Wine country, farm country, the bay.

From our front gate, you're 10 minutes from the western edge of the peninsula and under an hour from the lighthouse at Orient Point.

Wine Country

10–25 min drive

More than forty family-run vineyards stretch across the peninsula — tastings, cellar tours, and long lunches with bay views.

Farm Stands & Markets

Steps to 15 min

Heirloom tomatoes, sweet corn, oysters off the dock, sunflower fields, pumpkin patches, and homemade pies on the side of the road.

Harborside Villages

20–35 min drive

Walkable coastal towns with working harbors, ferry rides to neighboring islands, and tucked-away seafood shacks worth the line.

Beaches & State Parks

5–40 min drive

Calm bayside swimming, ocean-side surf, a county park next door, and two state parks within easy reach — one with a lighthouse.

Where We Are

Right in the middle of it all.

A working village on one side, a quiet sandy beach on the other, and the bay just steps away. Walking distance to dinner, drinks, and the water.

Jamesport Bay Suites67 Front Street
A 30-second walk to the bay. A 5-minute stroll to dinner. South Jamesport · NY · 11970

"Days here end the way they should — slowly, over the water."

By the Season

Every season has its own reason.

Whether you come for the harvest, the quiet, or the long beach days, the peninsula always has something waiting.

i.

Spring

Tulip fields in bloom, farm stands reopening, and the wineries pouring new releases. The crowds haven't arrived yet — and that's the point.

ii.

Summer

Long beach days, sailboat regattas, outdoor concerts at the vineyards, and oysters on the half shell. Sunset is mandatory.

iii.

Autumn

Harvest season at the wineries, pumpkin patches and corn mazes, foliage along the back roads, and apple cider straight from the press.

iv.

Winter

Cozy tasting rooms, holiday markets, lighthouse tours, and the kind of quiet shoreline you can have entirely to yourself.
